content.text:
The number of cosets of a subgroup $H$ in $G$ is called the 
**index** of $H$ in $G$. This quantity measures how "big" $G$ is 
relative to $H$ and plays a crucial role in many arguments.

content.title: Index of a Subgroup
content.content:
The **index** of $H$ in $G$, denoted 
$[G:H]$, is the number of distinct left cosets of $H$ in $G$.
From the proof of Lagrange's Theorem: $|G| = [G:H] \cdot |H|$.

content.title: Computing indices
content.problem:
- $[\Z_{12} : \langle 3 \rangle] = 12/4 = 3$

- $[S_4 : A_4] = 24/12 = 2$

- $[\Z : 5\Z] = \infty$ (but there are exactly $5$ cosets, so 
 we define $[\Z : 5\Z] = 5$)

content.text:
For infinite groups, we define the index as the cardinality of the set 
of cosets, which may be finite even when $G$ and $H$ are both infinite.

content.title: Infinite groups with finite index
content.problem:
$[\Z : n\Z] = n$ for any positive integer $n$. The cosets are 
$0 + n\Z, 1 + n\Z, \ldots, (n-1) + n\Z$.

content.content:
Let $\{g_i H : i \in I\}$ be the left cosets of $H$ in $G$, and 
$\{h_j K : j \in J\}$ be the left cosets of $K$ in $H$.

We claim the left cosets of $K$ in $G$ are exactly $\{g_i h_j K : i \in 
I, j \in J\}$.

Every $g \in G$ belongs to some $g_i H$, so $g = g_i h$ for some $h \in H$. 
Then $h \in h_j K$ for some $j$, so $g = g_i h_j k$ for some $k \in K$. 
Thus $gK = g_i h_j K$.

The cosets $g_i h_j K$ are distinct: if $g_i h_j K = g_k h_\ell K$, then 
$g_i h_j$ and $g_k h_\ell$ are in the same $K$-coset, so $g_i H = g_k H$ 
(hence $i = k$) and $h_j K = h_\ell K$ (hence $j = \ell$).

Thus $[G:K] = |I| \cdot |J| = [G:H] \cdot [H:K]$.

content.title: Index 2 Implies Normal
content.content: If $[G:H] = 2$, then $H$ is a normal subgroup of $G$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
There are exactly two left cosets: $H$ and $gH$ (for any $g \notin H$). 
Similarly, there are exactly two right cosets: $H$ and $Hg$.

Since the cosets partition $G$, we have $gH = G \setminus H = Hg$.

For any $g \in G$: if $g \in H$, then $gH = H = Hg$. If $g \notin H$, 
then $gH = G \setminus H = Hg$. Thus left and right cosets coincide, 
so $H$ is normal.

items:
Use the Tower Law to show that if $H \leq K \leq G$ and 
 $[G:H] = 30$, then $[G:K]$ divides $30$.
Prove that $A_n$ is a normal subgroup of $S_n$.

If $[G:H] = n$ and $[G:K] = m$ with $\gcd(n,m) = 1$, prove that 
 $[G : H \cap K] = nm$.

marginNotes.text:
Indeed, the very hairs of your head are all 
numbered.
